title: Backprop Diagnostic Checklist
concept: A backprop diagnostic checklist looks for signs like flat loss, NaNs, near-zero gradients, and layer updates that are frozen or wildly spiking.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a team use gradient clipping?

options.text: To keep overly large gradients from causing unstable giant updates.
To rename the optimizer after each batch.
To turn every hidden layer into an output layer.

exercises.explain: Right. Clipping is a safety check against destructive oversized updates.

cards.title: Like a mechanic's checklist
cards.body: When a car makes a strange noise, a mechanic does not guess wildly. They check the dashboard lights, oil level, brakes, and battery. Backprop debugging works the same way: when training feels wrong, you look for a few reliable signs before changing anything.

cards.title: What to check first
cards.body: A simple backprop checklist asks: Is the loss going down at all? Are gradients near zero, extremely huge, or NaN? Do some layers update while others stay frozen? These clues help you tell whether the gradient flow is healthy, vanishing, exploding, or broken in some other way.

cards.title: Taking the network's vital signs
cards.body: Doctors check pulse, temperature, and breathing before guessing at a cure. In training, the 'vital signs' are things like loss, gradient size, and whether weights are actually changing. Good diagnosis comes before random fixes.

cards.title: Reading the warning pattern
cards.body: If the loss is flat and early-layer gradients are almost zero, vanishing gradients are a strong suspect. If the loss spikes and gradients become huge or NaN, exploding gradients is a better suspect. The checklist helps connect symptoms to likely causes.

cards.title: Not every bad result means bad data
cards.body: It is easy to blame the dataset whenever training fails. Sometimes the real problem is the learning signal itself: maybe it faded away, blew up, or stopped reaching key layers. A quick gradient-flow check can save hours of guessing.

cards.title: Healthy signs vs. warning signs
cards.body: A checklist helps separate normal learning from trouble.

left.text:
Loss trends downward.
Gradients stay finite and useful.
Layers keep updating.

right.text:
Loss stays flat or spikes wildly.
Gradients are near zero, huge, or NaN.
Some layers freeze.

exercises.prompt: Which sign most strongly suggests exploding gradients?

options.text: The loss suddenly spikes and gradients become enormous or NaN.
The network has an input layer and an output layer.
Training examples are shown in batches.

exercises.explain: Correct. Sudden huge gradients and loss blow-ups are classic warning signs of exploding gradients.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If the loss stays flat for many steps and early layers barely update, gradient flow is worth checking.
exercises.explain: True. A flat loss plus frozen early layers often means the backward signal is not reaching where it needs to go.

exercises.sentence: If gradients turn into NaN, training has become ___.
exercises.explain: Yes. NaN gradients mean the training math has gone off the rails and needs investigation.

exercises.prompt: Which action best matches a backprop diagnostic checklist?

options.text: Check loss trend, inspect gradient sizes, and see whether different layers are actually updating.
Change ten settings at once without looking at the gradients.
Assume the data is broken before checking the training signals.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A checklist is about observing the training signals before guessing at a fix.

exercises.prompt: You inspect a model and see: loss barely changes, first layers have almost zero gradients, last layer gradients look normal, and no NaNs appear. According to the backprop checklist, what is the best diagnosis?

options.text: Likely vanishing gradients, because the early layers are not receiving enough backward signal.
Likely exploding gradients, because the gradients are too huge everywhere.
Likely perfect training, because no NaNs appeared.
Likely missing labels, because every flat loss is a data problem.

exercises.explain: Right. The checklist points toward vanishing gradients: the signal still reaches the last layer, but it fades before reaching the front of the network.
